perturbation.original_text: Report to the Reporting Entity within ten (10) business days of discovery any actual or suspected inappropriate use, disclosure , or breach of information from the Michigan Trauma Registry that involves data submitted under this Agreement ;
perturbation.changed_text: Report to the Reporting Entity within thirty (30) calendar days of discovery any actual or suspected inappropriate use, disclosure , or breach of information from the Michigan Trauma Registry that involves data submitted under this Agreement ;
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting timeframe to 30 calendar days may not align with HIPAA's breach notification requirements, which emphasize promptness.
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA requires notification without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days from discovery, but in many cases, a shorter timeframe is expected for proper mitigation. Requiring 30 calendar days may cause undue delay.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Breach Notification Rule | HHS.gov Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Navigate to: Breach Notification Rule The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR §§ 164.400-414, requires HIPAA covered entities and their business associates to provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. Similar breach notification provisions implemented and enforced by the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) , apply to vendors of personal health records and their third party service providers, pursuant to section 13407 of the HITECH Act. Definition of Breach A breach is, generally, an impermissible use or disclosure under the Privacy Rule that compromises the security or privacy of the protected health information. An impermissible use or disclosure of protected health information is presumed to be a breach unless the covered entity or business associate, as applicable, demonstrates that there is a low probability that the protected health information has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least the following factors: The nature and extent of the protected health information involved, including the types of identifiers and the likelihood of re-identification; The unauthorized person who used the protected health information or to whom the disclosure was made; Whether the protected health information was actually acquired or viewed; and The extent to which the risk to the protected health information has been mitigated. Covered entities and business associates, where applicable, have discretion to provide the required breach notifications following an impermissible use or disclosure without performing a risk assessment to determine the probability that the protected health information has been compromised. There are three exceptions to the definition of “breach.” The first exception applies to the unintentional acquisition, access, or use of protected health information by a workforce member or person acting under the authority of a covered entity or business associate, if such acquisition, access, or use was made in good faith and within the scope of authority. The second exception applies
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the reporting timeframe from 10 business days to 30 calendar days. The Perturbation Explanation states that this may not align with HIPAA's breach notification requirements, which emphasize promptness. The Law Explanation states that HIPAA requires notification without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days from discovery, but in many cases, a shorter timeframe is expected for proper mitigation and that requiring 30 calendar days may cause undue delay. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that HIPAA covered entities and their business associates must provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. While the 30-day timeframe is within the 60-day outer limit, the emphasis on 'promptness' and avoiding 'unreasonable delay' suggests a 30 calendar day timeframe may be seen as causing undue delay, which creates an inconsistency and a legal contradiction as it may not be compliant with HIPAA's requirements.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreeme nt may be terminated upon sixty days (60 ) written notice of the non - terminating party by the terminating party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreeme nt may be terminated upon thirty days (30) written notice of the non - terminating party by the terminating party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 30 days could violate Michigan's Public Health Code if it interferes with the reporting entity's legal obligations to provide data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Michigan Public Health Code, MCL 333.20101 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the 30-day notice period does not allow sufficient time for the reporting entity to fulfill its data submission obligations under the Public Health Code, the reduced notice period may be deemed unlawful interference.

(2) In addition, article 1 contains general definitions and principles of construction applicable to all articles in this code. History: 1978, Act 368, Eff. Sept. 30, 1978 ;-- Am. 1988, Act 332, Eff. No. 2003-1, compiled at MCL 445.2011. Popular Name: Act 368
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that reducing the termination notice period to 30 days could violate Michigan's Public Health Code if it interferes with the reporting entity's legal obligations to provide data. The law explanation further clarifies that if the 30-day notice period does not allow sufficient time for the reporting entity to fulfill its data submission obligations under the Public Health Code, the reduced notice period may be deemed unlawful interference. The original contract provides 60 days written notice, which would seemingly allow ample time to prepare necessary reporting data, while the changed text reduces the allotted time by half. This introduces an Inconsistency that creates a Legal Contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, MCL - Section 333.20101, mentions the Public Health Code, indicating its relevance. The altered text directly changes the timeframe allotted to the party responsible for terminating the agreement which is a time-sensitive obligation, and the altered time-frame of thirty days violates Michigan's Public Health Code (as mentioned in the Perturbation and Law Explanations), creating a Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Destroy all originals and copies of potentially identifiable information , in any format , in accordance with industry standards when no longer needed.
perturbation.changed_text: Destroy all originals and copies of potentially identifiable information , in any format, within 180 days of when it is no longer actively used.
perturbation.explanation: Setting a fixed destruction period of 180 days might conflict with HIPAA's data retention requirements, as HIPAA mandates retention for six years from the date of creation or last effective date, whichever is later, to permit compliance with accounting of disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA regulations require covered entities to retain documentation for six years. Destroying the information within 180 days would not allow for compliance with HIPAA's six-year retention period.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts HIPAA regulations regarding data retention. The original text requires destruction in accordance with industry standards, while the changed text sets a fixed 180-day destruction period. The Law Explanation confirms HIPAA mandates a six-year retention period, and the Perturbation Explanation highlights the potential conflict. Snippet 1 indicates HIPAA regulations are found in Title 45 of the eCFR, further solidifying the legal basis. Destroying information within 180 days, as specified in the changed text, would not comply with HIPAA's six-year retention mandate, therefore creating a contradiction. This aligns with the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition, as the change introduces a time-sensitive obligation that violates federal law, rendering the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that the unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information is punishable by imprisonment or fine or both under applicable state and federal laws specific to the data rel eased .
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that the unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information is punishable by a maximum fine of $1000 under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the penalty for unauthorized disclosure to a maximum fine of $1000 directly contradicts the significantly higher penalties possible under HIPAA and Michigan's Identity Theft Protection Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA establishes substantial penalties for violations, including significant fines and potential imprisonment, far exceeding the limited $1000 penalty suggested in the modified text.

General penalty for failure to comply with requirements and standards (a) General penalty (1) In general Except as provided in subsection (b), the Secretary shall impose on any person who violates a provision of this par
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits the penalty for unauthorized disclosure of confidential information to a maximum fine of $1000. This directly contradicts the penalties established by HIPAA, which include significant fines and potential imprisonment. The original text acknowledges the possibility of imprisonment or fine or both under applicable state and federal laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'General penalty for failure to comply with requirements and standards' under 42 USC 1320d-5, indicating that violations can result in penalties imposed by the Secretary. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is mostly a table of references, it points to the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (HIPAA). Limiting the penalty to $1000 contradicts the broader scope of penalties under HIPAA and other relevant laws, therefore, it falls under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The data provided to MDHHS will be used only for purposes of the Michigan Trauma Registry, consistent with Part 209 of the Public Health Code and the Michigan Administrative Code R 325.125 through 325.138.
perturbation.changed_text: The data provided to MDHHS may be used for the Michigan Trauma Registry and for any other purpose deemed necessary by MDHHS, notwithstanding Part 209 of the Public Health Code and the Michigan Administrative Code R 325.125 through 325.138.
perturbation.explanation: Expanding the data use beyond the explicitly defined purposes contradicts the limitations imposed by the Michigan Public Health Code and Administrative Code.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Michigan Public Health Code, MCL 333.20901 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Michigan Public Health Code outlines specific uses for trauma registry data. Stating that MDHHS can use the data for 'any other purpose' violates these restrictions and is illegal.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 333.20901 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 333.20901 Download Section Chapter 333 Act 368 of 1978 368-1978-17 368-1978-17-209 Previous Section Next Section PUBLIC HEALTH CODE (EXCERPT) Act 368 of 1978 333.20901 Meanings of words and phrases; general definitions and principles of construction. Sec. 20901. (1) For purposes of this part, the words and phrases defined in sections 20902 to 20908 have the meanings ascribed to them in those sections. (2) In addition, article 1 contains general definitions and principles of construction applicable to all articles in this code, and part 201 contains definitions applicable to this part. No. 2014-2, compiled at MCL 333.26253. For transfer of powers and duties of department of licensing and re
perturbation.justification: The original text limits data usage to the Michigan Trauma Registry, adhering to the Public Health Code. The changed text broadens the data usage to 'any other purpose deemed necessary by MDHHS,' explicitly overriding the limitations of Part 209 of the Public Health Code. This directly contradicts the legally defined purpose of the data as mentioned in the original contract. The scraped law snippet 1 references section 20901, which introduces definitions applicable to Part 209, suggesting that Part 209 outlines specific requirements and limitations regarding data usage, as claimed in the Law Explanation. Expanding data use beyond these defined purposes creates a legal contradiction.
